topic: A tenant receives an eviction notice
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, official paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I stared at the stark red lettering, my jaw tightening until a dull ache resonated in my molars. It was impossible. After five years of careful maintenance, of paying on time, of being a reliable resident, this was how it ended? I snatched the envelope, the sound of the tearing paper loud in the quiet apartment. My hands trembled, not from fear, but from a hot, churning frustration that made my chest feel tight and constricted. I paced the cramped living room three times, kicking at the worn rug. "This is a joke," I muttered, the words tasting metallic on my tongue. I wanted to shout, to dismantle the notice right there on the kitchen table, but I kept my voice low, a dangerous simmer beneath the surface.

[story 2]

I read the notice twice, slowly, deliberately, as if trying to memorize the precise phrasing designed to strip me of everything. A deep, slow burn started in my gut, spreading outward until my whole frame felt rigid. I slammed the door shut so hard the cheap frame rattled in its jamb. I leaned against the wood, breathing in short, ragged gasps. Every detail of the lease agreement flashed behind my eyes—the promises, the shared history—and it all felt like a deliberate mockery. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the call button, before slamming the device face down. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I clenched my fists until my knuckles were white, focusing all that internal pressure into a single, vibrating point.

[story 3]

The letter lay on the counter, an insult rendered in bureaucratic ink. I didn't bother to unfold it completely; I just stared at the address listed at the bottom, my gaze burning into the paper. A wave of intense heat washed over me, making the air feel thick and hard to draw into my lungs. I walked to the window, pulling the blinds shut with unnecessary force, plunging the room into shadow. My breath hitched, sharp and shallow. I turned away from the glass, my shoulders hunching forward as if bracing for an impact. I let out a long, slow exhale, a sound more like a suppressed growl than a breath, before turning back to the notice, my eyes narrowed to slits.
